items.title: Understanding Peritonitis: Causes and Pathogenesis
items.content: Peritonitis arises when a normal anatomic barrier in the peritoneal cavity is disrupted, allowing organisms to enter this normally sterile space. This can occur due to ruptures (e.g., appendix, diverticulum), weakening of the bowel wall by ischemia or inflammation, or adjacent inflammatory processes like pancreatitis or pelvic inflammatory disease.
keyPoints: Peritonitis often results from a disrupted anatomic barrier in the peritoneal cavity.
Common causes include ruptures (e.g., appendix, diverticulum), ischemia, tumor, inflammation, and adjacent inflammatory processes.
Pathogenesis involves leukocyte recruitment with early PMN influx followed by mononuclear cell migration, regulated by resident-cell chemokine synthesis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.title: Clinical Features of Peritonitis: Recognizing Red Flags
items.content: Peritonitis is a life-threatening condition often accompanied by bacteremia and sepsis syndrome. The peritoneal cavity's normal serous fluid has <30 g/L protein content and <300 WBCs/μL, primarily mononuclear cells. In bacterial infections, there is an early influx of PMNs followed by mononuclear cell migration.
keyPoints: Peritonitis can lead to life-threatening bacteremia and sepsis syndrome.
Normal peritoneal fluid contains <30 g/L protein (mainly albumin) and <300 WBCs/μL, primarily mononuclear cells.
Bacterial infections cause an early influx of PMNs followed by prolonged mononuclear cell migration.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.title: Diagnosis of Primary Bacterial Peritonitis in Cirrhotic Patients
items.content: Primary bacterial peritonitis (PBP) is diagnosed by identifying >250 PMNs/µL in the peritoneal fluid of cirrhotic patients with ascites and fever. It's vital to exclude secondary causes using imaging like contrast-enhanced CT.
keyPoints: Occurs most commonly in conjunction with liver cirrhosis, often due to alcoholism.
>250 PMNs/µL in peritoneal fluid is diagnostic for PBP.
Contrast-enhanced CT helps exclude intraabdominal sources of infection.
Blood cultures should be taken simultaneously as bacteremia frequently accompanies PBP.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Risk Factors for Primary Bacterial Peritonitis
items.content: PBP typically presents with fever in up to 80% of patients, often accompanied by nonlocalizing symptoms like malaise or encephalopathy. Risk factors include cirrhosis-related changes such as small-intestinal bacterial overgrowth.
keyPoints: Fever is reported in up to 80% of PBP cases.
Nonlocalizing symptoms and jaundice should prompt consideration of PBP.
Cirrhosis-associated alterations in gut microbiota increase risk.
Risk factors for multidrug-resistant infections include nosocomial origin and recent broad-spectrum antibiotic use.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.title: Microbiology and Treatment Considerations in Primary Bacterial Peritonitis
items.content: PBP is often caused by enteric gram-negative bacilli like E. coli, but recent trends show more gram-positive bacteria due to prophylactic quinolone use. A single organism is typically isolated.
keyPoints: Enteric gram-negative bacilli such as Escherichia coli are commonly encountered in PBP.
Increased prevalence of gram-positive organisms and ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae due to quinolone prophylaxis.
A single organism is typically isolated; multiple organisms including anaerobes suggest secondary peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.title: Initial Empirical Antibiotic Therapy for Primary Bacterial Peritonitis
items.content: Prompt empirical antibiotic therapy is crucial in managing primary bacterial peritonitis (PBP) due to its association with increased mortality when treatment is delayed. Initial coverage should target gram-negative aerobic bacilli and gram-positive cocci until culture results are available.
keyPoints: Gram's staining of peritoneal fluid often gives negative results in PBP.
Third-generation cephalosporins such as cefotaxime (2 g q8h IV) provide reasonable initial coverage if local prevalence of multidrug-resistant organisms is low.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics like piperacillin/tazobactam or ceftriaxone with vancomycin for MRSA colonization are alternatives.
Broader empirical coverage may be needed for nosocomially acquired PBP or patients with prior resistant infections.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.title: Role of Albumin in Reducing Mortality in Primary Bacterial Peritonitis
items.content: Administering albumin early in the treatment of PBP can reduce mortality, particularly in patients presenting with specific laboratory abnormalities.
keyPoints: Albumin (1.5 g/kg within 6 hours and 1.0 g/kg on day 3) has shown a mortality benefit for PBP patients.
Greatest benefit observed in patients with serum creatinine levels ≥1 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen levels ≥30 mg/dL, or total bilirubin levels ≥5 mg/dL.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.title: Prognosis and Prevention Strategies for Primary Bacterial Peritonitis
items.content: PBP is associated with significant morbidity and mortality, prompting strategies for prevention. Prophylactic antibiotics are recommended for high-risk patients to reduce recurrence.
keyPoints: In-hospital mortality was 17.6% in a 2018 study; 23% at 30 days and 37% at 90 days in another study.
Prevention includes considering chronic antibiotic prophylaxis for patients with high risk of PBP, such as those with low ascitic fluid protein levels and impaired renal function or liver failure.
Prophylactic regimens include fluoroquinolones or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole; rifaximin is being considered due to its non-absorption.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.title: Understanding Secondary Peritonitis Etiology and Pathophysiology
items.content: Secondary peritonitis occurs due to bacterial contamination from an intraabdominal viscus, often involving mixed flora with gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes. The severity of infection is influenced by the source's normal flora and chemical irritation potential.
keyPoints: Mixed flora predominance: Gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes, especially from colonic sources.
Chemical irritation varies: Ruptured gastric ulcers cause less bacterial burden compared to ruptured appendix due to pH differences.
Colonic content leakage: Anaerobic organisms account for 99.9% of bacteria in feces (10^11 anaerobes/g vs. 10^8 aerobes/g).
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Symptoms of Secondary Peritonitis
items.content: Symptoms in secondary peritonitis depend on the inciting event, with pain patterns varying based on the affected organ. Patients often exhibit guarding, tenderness, fever, and leukocytosis.
keyPoints: Pain localization: Depends on the source; e.g., epigastric pain from gastric ulcers, right lower quadrant in appendicitis.
Guarding and tenderness: Marked voluntary/involuntary guarding with rebound tenderness is common.
Systemic signs: Patients are typically febrile with marked leukocytosis and a left shift of WBCs.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach in Secondary Peritonitis
items.content: While peritoneal fluid cultures can aid diagnosis, imaging studies like abdominal CT are preferred for identifying the source of contamination. Surgical intervention may be necessary without prior imaging in unstable patients.
keyPoints: Peritoneal tap: Rarely used except in trauma cases to exclude hemoperitoneum.
Imaging: Abdominal CT is recommended if the patient is stable; unstable patients may need immediate surgery.
Culture reliability: Cultures from chronic drain sites are not reliable for defining infection etiology.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.title: Antibiotic Regimens for Secondary Peritonitis Based on Illness Severity
items.content: The treatment of secondary peritonitis involves early antibiotic administration targeting aerobic gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes. The choice of antibiotics depends on the anticipated flora and severity of illness, with different regimens recommended for community-acquired versus healthcare-associated infections.
keyPoints: For mild to moderate disease: broad-spectrum $\beta$-lactam/$\beta$-lactamase inhibitors (e.g., ticarcillin/clavulanate 3.1 g q4–6h IV) or fluoroquinolones plus metronidazole.
For ICU/hospital-acquired infections: antibiotics targeting resistant organisms like Pseudomonas aeruginosa, e.g., imipenem (500 mg q6h IV), meropenem (1 g q8h IV).
Eravacycline (1 mg/kg q12h IV) approved for complicated intraabdominal infections.
Ampicillin or vancomycin added to cephalosporin regimens for enterococcal coverage in very ill patients.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.title: Role of Surgical Intervention and Antibiotics in Secondary Peritonitis
items.content: Management of secondary peritonitis requires both surgical intervention to address the underlying cause and antibiotics to prevent complications such as abscess formation, wound infection, and distant spread of infection. The urgency and success of source control significantly impact survival rates.
keyPoints: Surgical intervention is crucial for addressing the inciting process in secondary peritonitis.
Antibiotics are essential to treat early bacteremia and prevent complications like abscess formation.
The choice of empiric antibiotics does not affect survival; urgency and success of source control do.
Recombinant human activated protein C (APC) is no longer recommended for sepsis or septic shock due to increased bleeding risk.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of CAPD-Associated Peritonitis
items.content: CAPD-associated peritonitis is primarily caused by skin organisms, similar to intravascular device-related infections. Diagnosis hinges on the presence of >100 WBCs/μL with >50% neutrophils in dialysate, though dwell time affects these values. In automated dialysis, PMN percentage is crucial for diagnosis when absolute WBC count may be misleading.
keyPoints: CAPD-associated peritonitis often involves skin organisms and can lead to discontinuation of CAPD.
Diagnostic criteria: >100 WBCs/μL with >50% neutrophils in dialysate, though dwell time affects these values.
For automated peritoneal dialysis, use PMN percentage for diagnosis if absolute WBC count is misleading.
In equivocal cases or clear effluent, a second exchange with at least 2-hour dwell time may be necessary.
Common organisms include Staphylococcus species (~45% of cases), with coagulase-negative staphylococci decreasing in frequency.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.title: Risk Factors and Organisms in CAPD-Associated Peritonitis
items.content: Diabetes is a risk factor for CAPD-associated peritonitis. The most common pathogens are Staphylococcus species, with S. aureus more prevalent among nasal carriers. Coagulase-negative staphylococci have decreased in frequency. Gram-negative bacilli and fungi like Candida can also cause infections.
keyPoints: Diabetes is a risk factor for CAPD-associated peritonitis.
Staphylococcus species are the most common pathogens (~45% of cases).
S. aureus prevalence increases among nasal carriers; it's common in exit-site infections.
Coagulase-negative staphylococci frequency has decreased recently.
Gram-negative bacilli and fungi such as Candida can also cause CAPD-associated peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.title: Empirical Antibiotic Therapy for CAPD Peritonitis
items.content: Empirical therapy for CAPD peritonitis targets S. aureus, coagulase-negative Staphylococcus, and gram-negative bacilli until culture results are available. The choice of antibiotics should be based on local resistance patterns.
keyPoints: Target organisms: S. aureus, coagulase-negative Staphylococcus, gram-negative bacilli
First-generation cephalosporin (e.g., cefazolin) for gram-positive bacteria; fluoroquinolone or third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftazidime) for gram-negative bacteria
In areas with high MRSA rates, use vancomycin instead of cefazolin
Broaden gram-negative coverage in resistant areas: aminoglycoside, ceftazidime, cefepime, or carbapenem
Consider broad coverage including vancomycin for septic physiology or exit-site infections
Intraperitoneal antibiotics administered continuously or intermittently (at least 6 hours per dose)
IV antibiotics added if patient is severely ill; doses adjusted for renal failure
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.title: Management and Duration of Antibiotic Therapy in CAPD Peritonitis
items.content: The clinical response to empirical treatment should be rapid. If no improvement is seen after 48-96 hours, collect new samples for cultures and consider catheter removal.
keyPoints: Rapid clinical response expected; reassess if no improvement after 48–96 hours
New samples collected for cell counts and cultures if no response
Catheter removal considered if no response after 48-96 hours
Typical antibiotic treatment duration: 14 days without exit-site/tunnel infection
For exit-site or tunnel infections, consider catheter removal and extend therapy up to 21 days
Immediate catheter removal in fungal infections
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.title: Role of Bacteroides fragilis in Intraabdominal Abscess Formation
items.content: B. fragilis is a key pathogen in intraabdominal abscess formation, acting alone to induce abscesses in animal models. Its virulence is attributed to its capsular polysaccharide complex, which localizes bacteria into abscesses by stimulating host immune responses.
keyPoints: B. fragilis accounts for 0.5% of normal colonic flora but is the most common anaerobic bloodstream isolate in intraabdominal infections.
The capsular polysaccharide complex, especially polysaccharide A, has zwitterionic characteristics that localize bacteria into abscesses by stimulating host immune responses.
Polysaccharide A adherence to mesothelial cells stimulates production of tumor necrosis factor α and intercellular adhesion molecule 1 in peritoneal macrophages.
CD4+ T lymphocytes are critical for immunity against abscess formation, with interleukin 10 downregulating the inflammatory response.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.title: Immune Response and Pathogenesis of Intraabdominal Abscesses
items.content: Intraabdominal abscesses represent both an infection and a host response to confine microbes. The pathogenesis involves B. fragilis virulence factors, particularly its capsular polysaccharide complex, which triggers immune mechanisms leading to abscess formation.
keyPoints: Abscesses contain viable infecting organisms and PMNs within a fibrous capsule, representing both an infection and a host response.
B. fragilis virulence factors, especially the capsular polysaccharide complex, are critical in abscess formation.
The process of abscess induction depends on T lymphocyte stimulation by zwitterionic polysaccharides, leading to leukoattractant cytokine and chemokine secretion.
CD4+ T cells play a crucial role in immunity against abscesses, with interleukin 10 production downregulating the inflammatory response.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.title: Common Sources and Locations of Intraperitoneal Abscesses
items.content: Intraperitoneal abscesses often result from fecal spillage, particularly from a colonic source such as an inflamed appendix. They can form in various locations including the omentum, mesentery, pelvis, psoas muscles, subphrenic space, and even within visceral organs like the liver.
keyPoints: 74% of intraabdominal abscesses are intraperitoneal or retroperitoneal
Most result from fecal spillage from a colonic source
Common locations include omentum, mesentery, pelvis, psoas muscles, subphrenic space, and visceral organs like the liver
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.title: Role of B. fragilis in Female Genital Tract Infections
items.content: B. fragilis is a significant pathogen in abscesses occurring in the female genital tract, whether as a primary infection or extending into the pelvic cavity or peritoneum.
keyPoints: B. fragilis is prominent among organisms isolated from genital tract abscesses
Not commonly found in normal vaginal flora
Less common in pelvic inflammatory disease and endometritis without an associated abscess
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis of Intraabdominal Abscesses
items.content: Abdominal CT is the most effective imaging modality for diagnosing intraabdominal abscesses, though ultrasonography and nuclear scans like indium-labeled WBCs and gallium can also be useful. PET scanning offers high resolution and should be considered.
keyPoints: Abdominal CT has the highest yield for diagnosing intraabdominal abscesses
Ultrasonography is useful for right upper quadrant, kidneys, and pelvis
Indium-labeled WBCs may have a greater yield near the bowel compared to gallium scans
PET scanning provides high resolution and should be considered
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.title: Management Algorithm for Intraperitoneal Abscesses
items.content: The management of patients with intraabdominal abscesses involves an algorithm that includes percutaneous drainage, broad-spectrum antibiotics targeting the organisms involved, and a drainage procedure if definitive abscesses are present. Antimicrobial therapy is adjunctive to drainage or surgical correction.
keyPoints: Algorithm for management by percutaneous drainage (Fig. 137-3).
Broad-spectrum antibiotics target aerobic gram-negative bacilli, facultative, and anaerobic organisms.
Cultures from drain sites are not reliable for defining infection etiology.
Drainage is generally required except in diverticulitis-associated abscesses.
Most clinical treatment failures due to failure to achieve source control via drainage.
Antibiotic duration may be limited to 4-5 days with adequate source control.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.title: Empirical Antibiotic Therapy for Intraabdominal Infections
items.content: Empirical antibiotic therapy should cover aerobic gram-negative, facultative, and anaerobic organisms due to the link between death in intraabdominal sepsis and gram-negative bacteremia. Even if anaerobes are not cultured, they must be covered.
keyPoints: Empirical therapy includes coverage for aerobic gram-negative bacilli, facultative, and anaerobic organisms.
Death in intraabdominal sepsis is linked to gram-negative bacteremia.
Anaerobes must be covered even if not cultured from specimens.
Therapy similar to that for secondary peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.title: Diagnosis and Presentation of Liver Abscesses
items.content: Liver abscesses are a significant cause of visceral abscesses, accounting for 13% of intraabdominal abscesses. They may present with fever as the most common symptom, but only half show hepatomegaly or right-upper-quadrant tenderness. Fever of unknown origin in elderly patients can be an indicator.
keyPoints: Liver abscesses constitute 13% of intraabdominal and 48% of visceral abscesses.
Fever is the most common presenting sign; only 50% have hepatomegaly or right-upper-quadrant tenderness.
Elevated serum alkaline phosphatase in 70%, leukocytosis in 77%, anemia in 50%, and hypoalbuminemia in 33% of patients.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.title: Imaging Studies for Liver Abscesses
items.content: Imaging is crucial for diagnosing liver abscesses. Ultrasonography, CT, indium-labeled WBC or gallium scan, and MRI are reliable methods, often requiring more than one study.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography, CT, indium-labeled WBC or gallium scan, and MRI are used for diagnosis.
More than one imaging study may be required.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.title: Etiology and Organisms in Liver Abscesses
items.content: Liver abscesses can arise from various sources, with organisms varying accordingly. Biliary infections often involve enteric gram-negative bacilli and enterococci, while pelvic sources may include anaerobes like B. fragilis.
keyPoints: Biliary tract disease is a common source; Klebsiella pneumoniae is notable in certain regions.
Pelvic sources show mixed flora including B. fragilis.
Hematogenous spread usually involves single organisms like S. aureus or Streptococcus milleri group.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.title: Why Drainage is Crucial in Liver Abscess Management
items.content: Drainage, either percutaneous or surgical, remains the cornerstone of therapy for liver abscesses. While medical management alone is gaining interest, it generally requires longer antibiotic courses and may not be suitable for all cases.
keyPoints: Percutaneous drainage can involve a pigtail catheter or pulse lavage device.

Factors predicting failure of percutaneous drainage include multiple abscesses, viscous contents, associated biliary disease, yeast presence, untreated obstructed biliary tree, and lack of response in 4–7 days.
Mortality rate with treatment is approximately 15%.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.title: How to Manage Candidal Liver Abscesses
items.content: Candidal liver abscesses are treated initially with liposomal amphotericin B or an echinocandin, followed by fluconazole. In stable patients with susceptible isolates, fluconazole alone may suffice.
keyPoints: Initial treatment: Liposomal amphotericin B (3–5 mg/kg IV daily) or an echinocandin.
Subsequent therapy: Fluconazole.
Fluconazole alone (6 mg/kg daily) can be used in clinically stable patients with susceptible isolates.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.title: Why High Clinical Suspicion is Needed for Splenic Abscesses
items.content: Due to their rarity and high fatality rate if untreated, splenic abscesses require a high degree of clinical suspicion. Diagnosis often occurs at autopsy, emphasizing the need for vigilance.
keyPoints: Incidence ranges from 0.14% to 0.7% in autopsy series.
Diagnosis made only at autopsy in 37% of cases.
Commonly associated with bacterial endocarditis and more prevalent in immunosuppressed patients or those with hematologic disorders.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.title: Standard Treatment for Splenic Abscesses
items.content: Splenectomy with adjunctive antibiotics is traditionally considered the best approach for complex, multilocular abscesses or multiple splenic abscesses due to high mortality rates. Percutaneous drainage may be an alternative for single, small (<3-cm) abscesses in patients at high surgical risk.
keyPoints: High mortality figures reported for splenic abscesses
Splenectomy with antibiotics is standard treatment for complex or multiple abscesses
Percutaneous drainage can be used for single, small (<3-cm) abscesses in patients at high surgical risk
Early diagnosis is crucial for successful treatment
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.title: Vaccination Post-Splenectomy
items.content: Patients undergoing splenectomy should be vaccinated against encapsulated organisms to prevent infections, specifically Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Neisseria meningitidis.
keyPoints: Post-splenectomy vaccination is necessary
Vaccines for Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Neisseria meningitidis are recommended
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis in Splenic Abscess Treatment
items.content: The most important factor in the successful treatment of splenic abscesses is early diagnosis, which can significantly impact patient outcomes.
keyPoints: Early diagnosis is crucial for successful treatment
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.title: Management of Perinephric and Renal Abscesses: Drainage and Antibiotics
items.content: The treatment for perinephric and renal abscesses involves drainage of pus and antibiotic therapy tailored to the organisms identified. Percutaneous drainage is typically successful for perinephric abscesses.
keyPoints: Drainage of pus is essential in treating perinephric and renal abscesses.
Antibiotic therapy should target the specific organism(s) recovered from cultures.
Percutaneous drainage is usually effective for perinephric abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Psoas Abscesses: CT Imaging and Surgical Intervention
items.content: Psoas abscesses can arise from hematogenous spread, contiguous intraabdominal or pelvic processes, or nearby bony structures. They are often associated with osteomyelitis. The most useful diagnostic tool is CT imaging. Treatment involves surgical drainage and antibiotics directed at the causative organisms.
keyPoints: Psoas abscesses may result from hematogenous spread, contiguous intraabdominal/pelvic processes, or nearby bony structures.
Associated osteomyelitis is common in psoas abscess cases.
CT imaging is the most useful diagnostic technique for psoas abscesses.
Treatment includes surgical drainage and antibiotics targeting the specific organisms.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.question: Which structure divides the upper and lower peritoneal cavities?
items.explanation: The transverse mesocolon divides the upper and lower peritoneal cavities.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.question: What is a common early response in leukocyte recruitment during bacterial infections of the peritoneum?
items.explanation: An early influx of polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs) occurs during bacterial infections.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.question: What is the normal protein content in peritoneal fluid?
items.explanation: The normal protein content in peritoneal fluid is <30 g/L.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.question: Which of the following is most commonly associated with primary bacterial peritonitis in adults?
items.explanation: Primary bacterial peritonitis occurs most commonly in conjunction with cirrhosis of the liver.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.question: What is a diagnostic criterion for primary bacterial peritonitis?
items.explanation: The finding of >250 PMNs/µL is diagnostic for primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.question: Which type of organisms are less frequently found in primary bacterial peritonitis compared to secondary peritonitis?
items.explanation: Anaerobes are found less frequently in primary bacterial peritonitis than in secondary peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.question: What factor is NOT typically associated with the development of primary bacterial peritonitis?
options: Defect in filtration function due to liver disease
items.explanation: Primary bacterial peritonitis develops without an intraabdominal source of infection.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.question: Which of the following antibiotics provides reasonable initial coverage in moderately ill patients with primary bacterial peritonitis if the local prevalence of multidrug resistant organisms is low?
options: Piperacillin/tazobactam (3.375 g q6h IV for adults with normal renal function)
items.explanation: Cefotaxime is recommended as reasonable initial coverage in moderately ill patients if the local prevalence of multidrug resistant organisms is low.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.question: Empirical antibiotic treatment for primary bacterial peritonitis should cover which types of bacteria until culture results become available?
options: Gram-negative aerobic bacilli and gram-positive cocci
items.explanation: Prompt therapy should cover gram-negative aerobic bacilli and gram-positive cocci until culture results are available.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.question: What is the recommended initial dose of albumin for patients with primary bacterial peritonitis who present with specific laboratory abnormalities?
options: 1.0 g/kg within 6 h and 1.5 g/kg on day 3
1.5 g/kg within 6 h and 1.0 g/kg on day 3
2.0 g/kg within 12 h and 1.0 g/kg on day 3
items.explanation: Albumin (1.5 g/kg of body weight within 6 h of detection and 1.0 g/kg on day 3) has been shown to provide a mortality benefit.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.question: Which antibiotic prophylaxis regimen is recommended for patients with cirrhosis and gastrointestinal bleeding until bleeding resolves?
items.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is recommended for patients with cirrhosis and gastrointestinal bleeding.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.question: Which type of bacteria predominates in secondary peritonitis when the source is colonic?
items.explanation: Secondary peritonitis from colonic sources typically involves a mixed flora with facultative gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes predominating.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.question: What is the primary cause of early death in secondary peritonitis?
options: Gram-negative bacillary sepsis and potent endotoxins
items.explanation: Early death in secondary peritonitis is primarily due to gram-negative bacillary sepsis and circulating endotoxins.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.question: What is the typical pH range of colonic contents that leak into the peritoneal cavity?
items.explanation: Colonic contents have a pH range of 7–8, which does not cause significant chemical peritonitis but leads to intense infection due to bacterial load.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.question: What is the normal flora ratio in the colon below the ligament of Treitz?
options: Equal numbers of aerobes and anaerobes
items.explanation: The normal flora below the ligament of Treitz contains ~10^11 anaerobic organisms/g and only 10^8 aerobes/g.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen is recommended for community-acquired infections associated with mild to moderate secondary peritonitis?
options: Broad-spectrum β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations or a combination of either a fluoroquinolone plus metronidazole.
Ceftazidime or ceftolozane/tazobactam with antifungal coverage.
items.explanation: Community-acquired infections associated with mild to moderate disease can be treated with broad-spectrum β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations or a combination of either a fluoroquinolone plus metronidazole.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended for patients in intensive care units and/or those with health care-associated infections to target more resistant gram-negative organisms?
options: Imipenem or meropenem, higher-dose piperacillin/tazobactam, cefepime, or ceftazidime plus metronidazole.
items.explanation: Patients in intensive care units and/or those with health care-associated infections should receive antibiotics targeting more resistant gram-negative organisms such as imipenem, meropenem, higher-dose piperacillin/tazobactam, cefepime, or ceftazidime plus metronidazole.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.question: For which condition is antifungal coverage warranted in the treatment of secondary peritonitis?
options: When there is growth of Candida species from a sterile site.
In all cases of secondary peritonitis.
Only when Enterococcus faecium is present.
If the patient has been treated with recombinant human activated protein C.
items.explanation: Antifungal coverage is warranted if there is growth of Candida species from a sterile site.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.question: What is the most common cause of peritonitis in patients undergoing continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis (CAPD)?
options: Endogenous bacteria from the gastrointestinal tract
Skin organisms migrating along the catheter
Fungal infections such as Candida species
items.explanation: CAPD-associated peritonitis usually involves skin organisms, similar to intravascular device-related infection.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.question: Which factor has been reported as a risk for CAPD-associated peritonitis in a study from Taiwan?
items.explanation: Diabetes was reported to be a risk factor for CAPD-associated peritonitis in the study.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.question: What improvement has decreased the incidence of peritonitis from one case per 9 months to one case per 24 months in CAPD patients?
options: Use of antiseptic solutions for dialysate
Improvements in equipment design, especially the Y-set connector
items.explanation: Improvements in equipment design, particularly the Y-set connector, have reduced peritonitis incidence.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.question: Which empirical antibiotics are recommended for CAPD peritonitis in areas with high rates of methicillin-resistant S. aureus?
items.explanation: Vancomycin should be used instead of cefazolin in areas with high rates of methicillin-resistant S. aureus.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.question: What is the typical duration of antibiotic treatment for CAPD peritonitis without exit-site or tunnel infection?
items.explanation: The typical duration of antibiotic treatment is 14 days for patients who lack exit-site or tunnel infection.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.question: When should new samples be collected for cell counts and cultures during empirical therapy for CAPD peritonitis?
options: After 48–96 hours if there is no clinical response
Only if the patient shows signs of septic physiology
items.explanation: If the patient has not responded after 48–96 hours of treatment, new samples should be collected.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.question: Which organism is most frequently isolated from intraabdominal infections and is especially prominent in abscesses?
items.explanation: B. fragilis is the anaerobe most frequently isolated from intraabdominal infections and is especially prominent in abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.question: What is a critical virulence factor of B. fragilis that contributes to abscess formation?
items.explanation: The capsular polysaccharide complex on B. fragilis is critical for abscess formation.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.question: Which cytokine produced by CD4+ T regulatory cells helps prevent abscess formation?
items.explanation: Interleukin 10 downregulates the inflammatory response, thereby preventing abscess formation.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.question: Which organism is prominently involved in infections of the female genital tract when associated with an abscess?
items.explanation: B. fragilis is prominently involved in infections of the female genital tract when associated with an abscess.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.question: What imaging modality has the highest yield for diagnosing intraabdominal abscesses?
items.explanation: Abdominal CT probably has the highest yield for diagnosing intraabdominal abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment if needle aspiration yields infected fluid in acute necrotizing pancreatitis?
options: Antibiotic therapy with surgical or percutaneous drainage
items.explanation: Infected fluid in acute necrotizing pancreatitis should be treated with antibiotic therapy in conjunction with surgical and/or percutaneous drainage.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.question: Which type of abscess is least likely to rupture?
items.explanation: Diverticular abscesses are least likely to rupture.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary reason for performing drainage in patients with intraabdominal abscesses?
options: To obtain cultures to define the etiology of infections
To achieve source control and prevent clinical treatment failures
To reduce the need for surgical intervention
To ensure coverage against anaerobic organisms
items.explanation: Most clinical treatment failures are due to failure to drain the abscess and thereby achieve source control.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.question: Why is empirical antibiotic therapy for intraabdominal infection similar to that for secondary peritonitis?
options: Because both conditions are caused by the same organisms
To ensure coverage against gram-negative aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic organisms
To reduce the duration of antibiotic treatment
Because cultures from drain sites are reliable in both cases
items.explanation: Empirical therapy for intra-abdominal infection needs to include adequate coverage of gram-negative aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic organisms.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.question: What is the typical duration of antibiotic treatment after achieving adequate source control in abdominal abscesses?
items.explanation: With adequate source control, antibiotic treatment may be limited to 4 or 5 days.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.question: What percentage of intraabdominal abscesses are visceral according to the study mentioned?
items.explanation: In one study, 26% of intraabdominal abscesses were identified as visceral.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.question: Which laboratory finding is the most reliable in diagnosing liver abscesses?
items.explanation: The single most reliable laboratory finding for liver abscess is an elevated serum concentration of alkaline phosphatase.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.question: Which organism is most frequently isolated in liver abscesses arising from pelvic and other intraperitoneal sources?
items.explanation: In liver abscesses arising from pelvic and other intraperitoneal sources, B. fragilis is the most frequently isolated organism.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.question: What imaging study is NOT mentioned as a reliable method for diagnosing liver abscesses?
items.explanation: MRI, along with ultrasonography and CT, is mentioned as a reliable imaging study; X-ray is not specifically noted for liver abscess diagnosis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.question: What is the mainstay of therapy for intraabdominal abscesses, including liver abscesses?
items.explanation: Drainage is the mainstay of therapy for intraabdominal abscesses, including liver abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.question: Which factor does NOT predict the failure of percutaneous drainage in liver abscess treatment?
options: Viscous abscess contents plugging the catheter
Lack of clinical response within 4–7 days
items.explanation: The presence of yeast and susceptibility to fluconazole do not predict failure; rather, lack of response or viscous contents do.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.question: What is the average mortality rate for liver abscess treatment despite appropriate management?
items.explanation: The mortality rate averages 15% despite treatment.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.question: Which therapy is often initiated for candidal liver abscesses?
items.explanation: Initial treatment of candidal liver abscesses often includes liposomal amphotericin B.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.question: What is considered the best treatment approach for complex, multilocular splenic abscesses?
items.explanation: Splenectomy with adjunctive antibiotics is the best approach for complex, multilocular splenic abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.question: Which patients should be vaccinated against encapsulated organisms after undergoing splenectomy?
options: Only those with a history of infections
items.explanation: All patients undergoing splenectomy should be vaccinated against encapsulated organisms.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.question: What is the most important factor in the successful treatment of splenic abscesses?
items.explanation: The most important factor in successful treatment is early diagnosis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.question: For which type of splenic abscess has percutaneous drainage been shown to be effective?
items.explanation: Percutaneous drainage has worked well for single, small (<3-cm) abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.question: Which diagnostic technique is most useful for identifying psoas abscesses?
items.explanation: CT is the most useful diagnostic technique for psoas abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.question: What organisms are most likely isolated from a psoas abscess with an intraabdominal or pelvic source in the United States?
options: A mixture of enteric organisms including aerobic and anaerobic gram-negative bacilli
items.explanation: A mixed enteric flora is the most likely etiology when the abscess has an intraabdominal or pelvic source.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.question: What is a common cause of psoas abscesses in patients with osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: S. aureus is most likely to be isolated when a psoas abscess arises from hematogenous spread or a contiguous focus of osteomyelitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.statement: Peritonitis can lead to bacteremia and sepsis syndrome.
items.explanation: Peritonitis is often accompanied by bacteremia and sepsis syndrome.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.statement: The pancreas is located within the peritoneal cavity.
items.explanation: The pancreas, duodenum, and parts of the colon are in the anterior retroperitoneal space.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses | PERITONITIS

items.statement: The presence of >250 PMNs/µL in peritoneal fluid is diagnostic for secondary peritonitis.
items.explanation: >250 PMNs/µL in peritoneal fluid is diagnostic for primary bacterial peritonitis, not secondary.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.statement: Primary bacterial peritonitis can occur without preexisting ascites.
items.explanation: Primary bacterial peritonitis virtually always develops in patients with preexisting ascites.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.statement: Gram's staining of peritoneal fluid often gives positive results in primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.explanation: Gram's staining of peritoneal fluid often gives negative results in primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.statement: Empirical coverage for anaerobes is necessary in the treatment of primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.explanation: Empirical coverage for anaerobes is not necessary in the treatment of primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.statement: A ruptured gastric ulcer releases high-pH contents that cause significant chemical peritonitis.
items.explanation: A ruptured gastric ulcer releases low-pH contents, which serve as a chemical irritant but do not cause significant chemical peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.statement: In secondary peritonitis, cultures from chronic drain sites are reliable for defining the etiology of infections.
items.explanation: Cultures from chronic drain sites in secondary peritonitis are not reliable for defining the infection's etiology.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.statement: Recombinant human activated protein C should be used for sepsis or septic shock outside randomized clinical trials.
items.explanation: Recombinant human activated protein C was withdrawn from the market in 2011 and should not be used for sepsis or septic shock outside of randomized clinical trials.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.statement: Surgery is rarely indicated in peritoneal bacterial infection (PBP) but may be life-saving in secondary peritonitis.
items.explanation: While surgery is rarely needed for PBP, it can be crucial for treating secondary peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Secondary Peritonitis

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.statement: Peritonitis is the most common reason for discontinuation of CAPD.
items.explanation: The passage states that peritonitis is indeed the most common reason for discontinuing CAPD.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.statement: CAPD-associated peritonitis is typically caused by multiple organisms.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that CAPD-associated peritonitis is usually caused by a single organism.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.statement: Intraperitoneal antibiotics for CAPD peritonitis can be administered continuously with each exchange.
items.explanation: Intraperitoneal antibiotics are given either continuously or intermittently.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.statement: For fungal infections in CAPD peritonitis, the catheter should be retained.
items.explanation: In fungal infections, the catheter should be removed immediately.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.statement: B. fragilis must act synergistically with a facultative organism to induce abscess formation.
items.explanation: B. fragilis can cause abscesses alone in animal models, unlike most other Bacteroides species.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.statement: Antibodies to the capsular polysaccharide complex enhance bloodstream clearance of B. fragilis.
items.explanation: Antibodies to the capsular polysaccharide complex do indeed enhance bloodstream clearance of B. fragilis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 1)

items.statement: Randomized controlled studies have demonstrated a benefit from preemptive antibiotics for patients with necrotizing pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Randomized controlled studies have not demonstrated a benefit from preemptive antibiotics for patients with necrotizing pancreatitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.statement: Imaging procedures such as CT, ultrasonography, and PET scanning are essential in diagnosing intraabdominal abscesses.
items.explanation: Scanning procedures like abdominal CT, ultrasonography, and PET scanning facilitate the diagnosis of intraabdominal abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.statement: Results of cultures from drain sites are reliable for defining the etiology of infections.
items.explanation: Results of cultures from drain sites are not reliable for defining the etiology of infections.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.statement: Abscesses associated with diverticulitis usually require surgical intervention.
items.explanation: Abscesses associated with diverticulitis usually wall off locally after rupture of a diverticulum, so that surgical intervention is not routinely required.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.statement: Amebic serologic testing gives positive results in more than 95% of cases.
items.explanation: Amebic liver abscesses show positive amebic serologic test results in over 95% of cases.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.statement: Anaerobes are commonly involved in liver abscesses arising from biliary infections.
items.explanation: Unless previous surgery has been performed, anaerobes are not generally involved in liver abscesses arising from biliary infections.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.statement: Percutaneous drainage requires a shorter hospital stay compared to open surgical drainage.
items.explanation: The average length of hospital stay for percutaneous drainage is almost twice that for open surgical drainage.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.statement: Streptococcal species are the most common bacterial isolates from splenic abscesses.
items.explanation: Streptococcal species are indeed the most common bacterial isolates from splenic abscesses.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.statement: The mortality rate for splenic abscesses is low due to effective treatment options.
items.explanation: High mortality figures are reported for splenic abscesses, indicating a high mortality rate.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.statement: Percutaneous drainage is not useful for patients with high surgical risk.
items.explanation: Percutaneous drainage may be useful for patients with high surgical risk.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.statement: Percutaneous drainage is usually successful for perinephric abscesses.
items.explanation: For perinephric abscesses, percutaneous drainage is usually successful.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.statement: Psoas abscesses cannot arise from hematogenous sources.
items.explanation: Psoas abscesses may arise from a hematogenous source.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses

items.assertion: Small-intestinal bacterial overgrowth is frequently present in advanced stages of liver cirrhosis.
items.reason: This condition has been linked with pathologic bacterial translocation and primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.assertion: Primary bacterial peritonitis is commonly associated with a mixed flora including anaerobes.
items.reason: Anaerobes are found less frequently in primary bacterial peritonitis than in secondary peritonitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because primary bacterial peritonitis typically involves a single organism, not mixed flora including anaerobes.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PRIMARY (SPONTANEOUS) BACTERIAL PERITONITIS

items.assertion: Patients with primary bacterial peritonitis usually respond within 72 hours to appropriate antibiotic therapy.
items.reason: Antimicrobial treatment can be administered for as little as 5 days if rapid improvement occurs and blood cultures are negative.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.assertion: Gastric acid suppression has been definitively proven to prevent primary bacterial peritonitis.
items.reason: Several observational studies raise concern that gastric acid suppression may increase the risk of PBP.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as no prospective studies have addressed whether avoidance of such therapy may prevent PBP.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Primary Bacterial Peritonitis

items.assertion: Patients with secondary peritonitis often exhibit marked voluntary and involuntary guarding of the anterior abdominal musculature.
items.reason: This is due to increased pain from infection involving the parietal peritoneum, which is extensively innervated.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why guarding occurs in secondary peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.assertion: Abdominal CT is not useful for identifying the source of peritoneal contamination.
items.reason: Emergent studies like abdominal CT are recommended if the patient is hemodynamically stable to find the source of contamination.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because abdominal CT is indeed useful, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · SECONDARY PERITONITIS

items.subtopic: PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS
items.assertion: The finding of more than one organism in dialysate culture should prompt evaluation for secondary peritonitis.
items.reason: Multiple organisms in the dialysate suggest a potential breach in the peritoneal barrier, indicating possible secondary peritonitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why multiple organisms prompt evaluation for secondary peritonitis.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · PERITONITIS IN PATIENTS UNDERGOING CONTINUOUS AMBULATORY PERITONEAL DIALYSIS

items.assertion: Vancomycin is included in empirical therapy for CAPD peritonitis if there's a history of methicillin-resistant S. aureus infection.
items.reason: Vancomycin should be used instead of cefazolin in areas with high rates of methicillin-resistant S. aureus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · CAPD Peritonitis

items.assertion: Imipenem is frequently used for preemptive antibiotics in necrotizing pancreatitis.
items.reason: It reaches high tissue levels in the pancreas, which is beneficial for treating infections.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain why imipenem is used despite studies showing no benefit from preemptive antibiotics.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.assertion: Exploratory laparotomy is commonly performed to diagnose intraabdominal abscesses.
items.reason: CT and other imaging techniques have largely replaced the need for exploratory laparotomy in diagnosing abscesses.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because exploratory laparotomy is less commonly used, but it may still be necessary if an abscess is strongly suspected.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · INTRAPERITONEAL ABSCESSES (part 2)

items.assertion: Empirical antibiotic therapy for intraabdominal infection should cover gram-negative aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic organisms.
items.reason: Death in intraabdominal sepsis is linked to gram-negative bacteremia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why empirical therapy must cover these organisms.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Intraperitoneal Abscesses

items.assertion: Klebsiella pneumoniae is linked to a specific genotype and hypermucoviscous phenotype.
items.reason: This syndrome includes liver abscess, bacteremia, and metastatic infection in the eye(s) and lung(s).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason does not explain why Klebsiella pneumoniae is linked to a specific genotype.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.assertion: Fever of unknown origin may be the only manifestation of liver abscess.
items.reason: This is especially common in elderly patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why fever of unknown origin can be a sole manifestation.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · VISCERAL ABSCESSES

items.assertion: Empirical antibiotic therapy for liver abscess should be adjusted based on Gram's staining and culture results.
items.reason: Blood cultures and diagnostic aspirate of abscess contents are obtained before initiating empirical therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.assertion: Splenomegaly is found in all cases of splenic abscess.
items.reason: Splenic abscesses often present with abdominal pain localized to the left upper quadrant.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; splenomegaly is found in only ~50% of cases.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Liver Abscesses

items.assertion: Splenectomy is the standard treatment for splenic abscesses.
items.reason: It remains the best approach for complex, multilocular abscesses or multiple abscesses.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.assertion: Percutaneous drainage is effective for all types of splenic abscesses.
items.reason: It has been shown to work well for single, small (<3-cm) abscesses in some studies.
items.explanation: A is false because percutaneous drainage is not effective for complex, multilocular or multiple abscesses; R is true.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Splenic Abscesses

items.subtopic: Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
items.assertion: S. aureus is the most likely organism isolated in psoas abscesses arising from hematogenous spread.
items.reason: Psoas abscesses are often caused by S. aureus when there is a contiguous focus of osteomyelitis or hematogenous spread.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Intraabdominal Infections and Abscesses · Perinephric and Renal Abscesses | Psoas Abscesses
